Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place this semester. I tried so hard to create schedules and stick to them, but between my extracurriculars and everything else going on, things just fell apart constantly. I'd make these detailed plans on Sunday nights, color-coded and everything because I'm really visual, but by Tuesday I'd already be behind. I spent about 11 hours a week studying, but it felt scattered and panicked rather than organized.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling that week. Some weeks I'd try to study at the same times, but then a deadline would hit or I'd have a club commitment, and everything would shift. I found myself doing most of my work in these intense bursts right before things were due. It wasn't sustainable, and I knew that, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle no matter how hard I tried.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was either panic about a deadline or seeing a notification that something was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which honestly made my anxiety worse because I'd see all these things I hadn't done yet. The fear of falling behind was a big motivator, but not in a healthy way. I'd also log in when I felt guilty about not engaging enough.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a comfortable routine for me. There were maybe a week or two where I logged in daily, but then something would throw me off—a bad grade, feeling overwhelmed, or just exhaustion—and I'd avoid it for days. The LMS kind of became this source of stress rather than a helpful tool. Every time I opened it, I saw everything I was failing to keep up with.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly lifesavers for me because I learn so much better visually. I watched almost 290 minutes of video content because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb things at my own pace. The discussion boards were also helpful because seeing other students' questions made me feel less alone in my confusion. When I could actually engage with those resources without feeling rushed, I learned a lot.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources because I just didn't have time and they weren't visual enough for me. The dense text-based content felt impossible to get through when I was already stressed and pressed for time. I also delayed a lot of the smaller assignments, which is why my completion rate was so low. I kept telling myself I'd catch up, but I never did.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my grades or when it actually helped me understand something I was struggling with. If it was going to be graded, I prioritized it, even if I did it last minute. The peer interactions and discussions felt valuable too because they helped me process the material and feel less isolated in my struggles.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When I saw something was optional, my brain immediately filed it under 'skip this' because I was barely keeping up with required work. It's frustrating because I know some of those optional resources probably would have helped me understand things better, but the anxiety of incomplete required tasks made it impossible to justify spending time on extras.
Q9: Yes, definitely. Since I don't have reliable internet access at home, I downloaded a lot of materials when I was on campus and studied them offline. I'd print out slides and annotate them by hand, or screenshot important diagrams to study from my phone later. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed notes spread across my bed, not logged into anything.
Q10: There were so many times I was studying intensely but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours going through my printed notes, rewatching downloaded videos, or working through problems in a notebook. I also studied a lot with a friend from class—we'd quiz each other and explain concepts—but none of that shows up anywhere. My exam score was decent because of that offline work.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, take screenshots of the key diagrams, and then study those images repeatedly offline. Or I'd download a PDF, print it, and never open it on the LMS again even though I reviewed it multiple times. My actual engagement with the material was way higher than my login frequency would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially around midterms when everything piled up at once. My anxiety got really bad—I felt scared constantly, like I was drowning and couldn't catch my breath. During those times, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because logging in just reminded me of how behind I was. The CBT sessions helped a little, but the fear never fully went away.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from extracurriculars or overwhelmed by multiple deadlines, I'd barely touch the LMS. I'd do the bare minimum—check what was due, maybe skim something—but I wasn't really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities were huge because I couldn't drop my other commitments, so coursework often got squeezed into whatever time was left, which wasn't much.
Q14: Yes, more times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about other deadlines or just too tired to focus. I'd watch videos but realize at the end that I hadn't retained anything. It was like going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The fear of doing them poorly made me avoid starting them at all. It's this terrible cycle where the anxiety about the task makes you avoid it, which creates more anxiety. I'd tell myself I'd feel more ready tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel even more scared because now there was less time.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have this burst of panicked activity right before something was due, cramming and rushing to get anything turned in. My LMS usage probably spiked dramatically in the 24 hours before deadlines. It wasn't healthy, but without that pressure, I couldn't seem to motivate myself to start things.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but negative feedback or low grades made me want to avoid the course entirely. After getting a disappointing score, I'd sometimes not log in for days because I felt too defeated. The fear of seeing more bad news made me hesitant to even check my grades.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming for me. Having three online courses simultaneously meant there was always something due somewhere, and I couldn't keep track of it all. The way materials were organized in the LMS wasn't always intuitive, so I'd waste time just trying to find what I needed. A clearer, more visual layout would have helped me so much.
Q19: The lack of visual organization made engagement harder for me. I learn best when I can see the big picture—timelines, flowcharts, clear pathways through the material. When everything was just lists of links and text, I felt lost. The discussion boards were actually designed well and made participating easier, which is why I posted there fairly often.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking through things in a panic, but not actually completing work because the fear of doing it wrong is paralyzing. You might see high activity because they're checking, rechecking, watching videos multiple times out of stress, but the actual submissions don't happen because starting feels impossible.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. My exam score was 74, which is decent, even though my assignment completion was really low. I did a lot of learning offline—studying printed materials, working with a friend, reviewing downloaded content. The LMS didn't capture that effort at all. Someone could be genuinely engaged with the material without it showing in their login data.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything or how much you studied offline. They definitely can't capture the emotional experience—the anxiety, the fear, the moments of breakthrough understanding. They miss all the learning that happens away from the screen, which for me was a lot.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, engagement also means feeling emotionally invested, even when that investment comes with a lot of fear and stress. It's not just about showing up—it's about being mentally present.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed notes, the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the genuine learning that happened in study sessions with my friend. It also wouldn't show how much I actually cared about doing well, even when my completion rates suggested otherwise. My struggle and my effort are invisible in those numbers.
